text:
Admission Date:  1987-05-03     Discharge Date: 1995-04-22

Date of Birth:   2017-05-07     Sex:  M

Service:  MICU

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  The patient is a 70-year-old
white male who was in his usual state of health at his
nursing home until one day prior to admission when he vomited
partially digested food.  He was assessed at that time, put
on a clear liquid diet and given Dulcolax for possible
obstipation without effect.  On the day of admission, the
patient had coffee-grounds emesis with a decreased blood
pressure of 102/50, and an increased heart rate of 102, with
mild right upper quadrant epigastric tenderness.  The patient
was sent to Sycamore Springs Emergency
Fairview Hospital for further evaluation.

In the Emergency Department, the patient had two large bore
intravenous lines placed and was started on Protonix.  He was
uncooperative and found to have decreased oxygen saturations
on his nonrebreather mask and was intubated to control his
airway and for hypoxia given his oxygen saturations at that
time.  The patient had a Foley, an NG-tube, and a right
internal jugular triple lumen catheter placed, and was given
a total of 8 mg of Ativan.  The patient also received
levofloxacin and Flagyl prior to blood cultures being drawn.
He was lavaged 750 cc, which was positive for coffee-grounds
emesis.  The patient did not have any bright red blood per
the lavage.  He received a total of 7 liters of normal saline
while in the Emergency Department.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
 1.  Alcohol-related dementia with psychosis; no known
definite varices or cirrhosis.
 2.  Depression.
 3.  Hypertension.
 4.  Complete heart block; status post pacemaker placement
in 1983.
 5.  Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
 6.  Hypercholesterolemia.
 7.  Goiter.
 8.  Positive purified protein derivative; treated with INH
times six months that started in 1981-06-21.
 9.  Chronic renal insufficiency with a baseline creatinine
of 1.4.
10.  Pancreatic insufficiency and zinc deficiency.

MEDICATIONS ON ADMISSION:  The patient's medications on
admission included Lipitor 10 mg p.o. q.d., Viokase one
tablet p.o. t.i.d., Depakote 250 mg p.o. t.i.d., Metamucil
one packet p.o. q.d., aspirin 81 mg p.o. q.d., multivitamin
one tablet p.o. q.d.

ALLERGIES:  The patient has no known drug allergies.

SOCIAL HISTORY:  The patient is a resident at Abrazo West Campus Home since 1995.  The patient is baseline ambulatory
and independent with activities of daily living except for
hygiene.  The patient does have a history of tobacco use, but
no known recent alcohol use.  The patient's next of Michael are
his sisters, Kenneth Jackson II Jessica Robinson, Crystal Sheila Bates.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION ON PRESENTATION:  The patient's physical
examination on admission revealed blood pressure was 110/60,
heart rate was 110, oxygen saturation was 100%.  In general,
he was sedated, intubated, elderly white male in no apparent
distress.  Head, eyes, ears, nose, and throat examination
revealed dry mucous membranes.  The neck was supple, thyroid
nodule.  Heart had a regular rate and rhythm.  S1, S2, no
murmurs, gallops or rubs.  Some soft heart sounds.  Lungs
were clear to auscultation anteriorly.  The abdomen was soft,
nontender, protuberant.  Slightly decreased bowel sounds.
Extremities were cool and dry with 2+ dorsalis pedis pulses.
No edema.  Neurologically, sedated and not responsive.  Skin
revealed venous stasis changes on the lower extremities
bilaterally.

PERTINENT LABORATORY DATA ON PRESENTATION:  Laboratories on
admission revealed white blood cell count was 8.2 (with
neutrophils of 37%, bands of 43%, lymphocytes of 17%,
monocytes of 17%, eosinophils of 1%, basophils of 0%),
hematocrit was 42.4, platelets were 245.   PT was 12.7, PTT
was 25.5, INR was 1.1.

Chemistries revealed sodium was 140, potassium was 4.3,
chloride was 99, bicarbonate was 25, blood urea nitrogen
was 35, creatinine was 1.6, and blood glucose was 134.  ALT
was 10, AST was 22, CK was 202, alkaline phosphatase was 79,
amylase was 261, total bilirubin was 0.6, lipase was 9.

CK/MB was 5.  Creatine kinase was 202.  Troponin was less
than 0.3.  Protein was 7.7, albumin was 4.2.

Urinalysis was significant for trace blood with 0 to 2 red
blood cells; otherwise negative.

Cultures were all sent.  An arterial blood gas showed pH of
7.32, PCO2 was 44, and PO2 was 65.

RADIOLOGY/IMAGING:   An electrocardiogram was atrial sensed
and ventricular paced rhythm.

ASSESSMENT:  This is a 70-year-old male with a history of
alcohol-related dementia and chronic renal insufficiency who
presented with a 1-day history of coffee-grounds emesis,
hypertension, tachycardic, and likely aspiration pneumonia.

HOSPITAL COURSE BY SYSTEM:
1.  GASTROINTESTINAL:  The patient presented with an upper
gastrointestinal bleed with negative clearance with a
nasogastric lavage.  His initial hematocrit fell 10 points;
however, after this initial decline, the patient had no
further evidence of a gastrointestinal bleed.  The patient
was not scoped initially by Gastroenterology who felt that
there was no acute necessity for endoscopy.  In addition, the
patient was on multiple pressors, and the Gastroenterology
team felt that unless he had an acute event he did not need
an immediate esophagogastroduodenoscopy.

The patient was eventually scoped after his pressure
stabilized.  The esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed grade 2
esophagitis in the lower third of the esophagus; erythema
suggestion, and mosaic appearance in the stomach body and
fundus that was consistent with portal gastropathy; erythema
and friability in the anterior bulb, distal bulb, and
posterior bulb that was consistent with duodenitis.
Otherwise, a normal esophagogastroduodenoscopy to the third
part of the duodenum.

Gastroenterology felt that there was no clear evidence of
what caused the patient's initial presentation.  Their only
recommendations included continuing Protonix and avoiding
aspirin use.

2.  INFECTIOUS DISEASE:  The patient likely had evidence of
aspiration pneumonia given recent coffee-grounds suctioning
from the endotracheal tube.  The patient was started on
antibiotics to cover gram-negative as well as anaerobes.  The
patient continued to do well on this regimen.  Follow-up
cultures showed methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
positive sputum, and the patient was started on vancomycin.

The patient's chest x-ray improved with no clear infiltrate.
He remained afebrile with no leukocytosis.  The patient's
initial bandemia on presentation quickly resolved with
antibiotics.  The patient's initial hypotensive picture
seemed to have a septic component, and this also resolved
with antibiotics.

3.  HYPOTENSION:  The patient was maintained on intravenous
fluids and was pressor dependent initially in his hospital
stay.  The patient's urine output was monitored to assess for
adequate and organ perfusion.  The patient's volume status
was unclear as his hypotension persisted for over one week.
A Swan-Ganz catheter was placed which helped differentiate
the patient's hypotension etiology.  It was likely the
patient had a component of both hypovolemia as well as
distributive shock.  Both of these with treatment including
fluids and antibiotics, helped resolve the hypotension with
eventual weaning from his pressors.

4.  RESPIRATORY FAILURE:  The patient was intubated secondary
to airway protection and hypoxia that was suggested by both
his arterial blood gas and oxygen saturations.  The patient
was extubated without initial complications.  The patient did
have some increased secretions and congestion although he was
noted to have a very good cough, and no further action was
taken.  The patient was suctioned p.r.n.

5.  CARDIOVASCULAR:  The patient was initially ruled out for
myocardial infarction given his presentation of hypotension.
An echocardiogram was done while in house to assess his
ejection fraction, which was determined to be 20%.  The
patient was monitored on telemetry throughout his hospital
stay.

6.  RENAL:  The patient's urine output remained stable
throughout his hospital stay.  His creatinine remained
between 1.0 and 1.4.  The patient tolerated fluid boluses and
diuresis as necessary.

7.  HEMATOLOGY:  The patient required 2 units of packed red
blood cells transfusion.  The patient's drop in hematocrit
was unclear given negative blood in his stool or emesis.  The
patient's hematocrit remained stable after his transfusion.

8.  NEUROLOGY/PSYCHIATRY:  The patient was continued on his
valproic acid which was his outpatient regimen.  The patient
became slightly aggressive when he was initially weaned from
his sedation, although eventually the patient responded well
to valproic acid and Ativan as needed.

9.  DISPOSITION:  The Kindred Hospital Riverside health care proxies (both of
his sisters) determined the patient to be do not
resuscitate/do not intubate after he was extubated.  The
patient's sisters goals were to keep the patient from any
suffering and were explicit in their desires for no
reintubation for any reason.


NOTE:  There will be an addendum to this Discharge Summary
once the patient is transferred to the floor.



Dictated By:Scott Williams
MEDQUIST36

D:  1976-03-02  14:55
T:  1995-04-22  20:01
